Joseph's life in prison has not broken his trust in God. When two desperate men come to him with troubling dreams, Joseph immediately directs them to the true source of interpretation: "Don't interpretations belong to God?" He does not set himself up as a wise interpreter with special powers, but as a man who knows that God alone reveals what is hidden. This simple confidence reveals something crucial about Joseph. Slavery, false accusation, and years of captivity have not embittered him or made him cynical about God's existence or care. Instead, they have deepened his conviction that God speaks and acts, even in an Egyptian prison. He offers his gift freely, asking only that the cupbearer remember him—a modest request from a man who has every reason to feel forgotten.

But Joseph's interpretation itself cuts deeply. The three branches and three baskets both mean three days, but the outcomes could not be more different. For the cupbearer, restoration and honor. For the baker, death. Both men stand in the presence of the same God, both receive their answer from the same servant, yet their fates diverge completely. This is not a story about positive thinking or the power of dreams. Joseph is announcing what will happen, not because he possesses magical insight, but because God has revealed it to him. The words come true exactly as spoken. The baker is executed; the cupbearer is restored.

And then comes the hard ending. The very man Joseph asked to remember him forgets him completely. After three years in prison, after demonstrating his God-given ability to interpret dreams, after asking for one small kindness, Joseph remains abandoned. The text lingers on this detail: "The chief cupbearer, however, did not remember Joseph; he forgot all about him." This is not incidental. It shows that Joseph's obedience and faithfulness do not guarantee immediate relief or recognition. He spoke truth, served well, asked humbly—and was left in prison anyway. Yet the chapter does not end with Joseph's despair. It ends with the fact of his abandonment, which forces us to ask: what holds a man's trust when his only hope—the cupbearer—proves worthless? The answer must be something deeper than human gratitude or the machinery of earthly justice.

What Joseph possessed then, and what sustains him still, is knowledge that God rules over kingdoms and interprets all things. He did not interpret those dreams to secure his release; he interpreted them because he belonged to a God who speaks truth into the darkness. Joseph would wait two more years in that prison, the cupbearer's forgetfulness a scar that reminds us that God's faithfulness operates on a different timeline than our own. Trust in God means knowing that His purposes will be accomplished, not that people will remember us or reward us when we deserve it.
